nativeRange.habitat: Coastal lagoons and sheltered coral reefs at depths of roughly 2-30 m, perching and grazing among rocks and rubble.

diet.notes: The Tailspot Blenny is primarily a grazer, picking at film algae and short turf algae that grow naturally on live rock. It is generally advised to supplement this natural grazing with herbivore prepared foods such as spirulina-based flakes or pellets, nori sheets clipped near the rockwork, and occasional small frozen meaty items such as mysis or enriched brine shrimp to help maintain condition once tank algae thin out.

avoidWith: large or aggressive species that may intimidate it
other Ecsenius species in small tanks

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 1916x1437; sharp full-body in-situ shot clearly showing the diagnostic dark white-ringed caudal-base spot, orange eye-ring and blue-to-olive body colour on a vibrant live individual.
desertnaturalist; 1973x1207; full-body in-situ view showing the tail spot and blue head; colourful but slightly softer than the recommended pick.
Rickard Zerpe; 2449x1837; crisp, vibrant head-and-body portrait showing the blue head and black-and-yellow eye band, but the caudal spot is out of frame, so kept as a non-recommended extra.

summary: Small Western Pacific reef blenny with a dark caudal-base spot, generally considered a peaceful, personable algae grazer suited to nano and small reef tanks.
careNotes: Recognised by a dark spot at the caudal-fin base and a black-and-yellow band below each eye; reaches roughly 5-6 cm. Generally considered peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but can be territorial toward other blennies, so usually kept singly unless the tank is large with well-separated rockwork. Tends to perch on live rock and graze on film and turf algae; supplementing with herbivore prepared foods and nori is commonly recommended to keep condition up once tank algae thin out. Has been known to jump, so a tightly-fitted lid or covered overflows are advised. Generally regarded as reef-safe; isolated reports of nipping at small polyps exist but are uncommon.
breedingNotes: The Tailspot Blenny is oviparous, spawning with a distinct paired partner. Eggs are demersal and adhesive, secured to a hard substrate, and the resulting larvae are planktonic. Captive breeding is uncommonly reported for this species.
